entities.name: vaikharī
entities.cite: vaikhari, the elaborated — audible, articulated, sequence-ridden speech

entities.name: madhyamā
entities.cite: madhyama, the middle — the inner word, mentally deployed, sequenced in thought though unsounded

entities.name: paśyantī
entities.cite: pasyanti, the seeing — speech at the station where it *sees* rather than says: undivided, sequence-swallowed, intentionality without articulation

entities.name: param padam
entities.cite: beyond even the triad, a supreme station of speech

entities.name: śabda
entities.cite: the sabda-tattva of 1.1 stands at (or as) the summit of an accessible gradient

entities.name: tīrtha
entities.cite: each level has many fords, many descents and crossings

rejected_readings.label: para_vac_fourth_level
rejected_readings.rendering: Four entirely distinct and non-hierarchical levels of speech exist: vaikhari, madhyama, pasyanti, and a separate para-vac level.
rejected_readings.why: The verse speaks of the 'param padam of the threefold vac', suggesting a summit or culmination of the three-level hierarchy rather than an additional level standing apart from them. The commentary leaves this ambiguous on purpose—whether param padam is pasyanti's absolute aspect or the later-systematized para-vac—but the karika's syntax supports the minimal reading of param padam as the pinnacle of the existing three.

rejected_readings.label: denies_hierarchy
rejected_readings.rendering: Vaikhari, madhyama, and pasyanti are three unrelated modes of speech with no connection between them.
rejected_readings.why: The commentary establishes each as a stage in a unified gradient: 'the sabda-tattva of 1.1 stands at the summit of an accessible gradient — which makes the return-path of 1.131 (union by the word's perfecting) a climb through one's own speaking, from voice through mind to vision.' This explicit description of ascent through the levels contradicts a reading that treats them as disconnected.

rejected_readings.label: denies_supremacy
rejected_readings.rendering: The three levels of speech—vaikhari, madhyama, and pasyanti—constitute the final, ultimate reality; there is no station beyond them.
rejected_readings.why: The verse's central assertion, marked as 'adbhuta' (wonder), is precisely that there IS a supreme station (param padam) beyond the threefold speech. To deny the existence of param padam directly contradicts the verse's primary claim.

notes: The contract formalizes 1.142's declaration of three hierarchically-arranged levels of speech (vaikhari, madhyama, pasyanti), each with multiple modes (aneka-tirtha-bheda), and the existence of a supreme station (param padam) that transcends them and identifies with sabda-tattva. The verse's ambiguity—left open by the commentary—between param padam as pasyanti's absolute aspect vs. a fourth para-vac level is not resolved in the axioms; instead, the contract encodes the 'minimal reading' the commentary prefers (that param padam is the summit of the three), while the denied reading avoids commitment to para-vac as a separate level.
